revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]の[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びか...'
revision.text:
'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]の[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。 ==概要== [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。
「要領」の推進者として[[森戸辰男]]文部大臣の名があげられる<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html 森戸辰男、人と思想 小池聖一]</ref>。
「要領」には、「祖国再建をめざす積極的な意欲と情熱にみちた力強く新しい精神」、「自立自救の責任感と相互扶助の友愛心」「生活の窮苦に打ち克つ心構え」、「明日の正しく明るく力強い文化日本」<ref name="shin"/>など、国民の精神・文化の発揚、自己啓発を強く呼びかけるフレーズが並んだ。
「要領」では、「文化の面における新日本建設の力強い行進」を喚起するもので、「要領」には、「勤労意欲の高揚」「[[友愛]]協力の発揮」「自立精神の養成」「社会正義の実現」「合理的・民主的な生活慣習の確立」「[[芸術]]、[[宗教]]および[[スポーツ]]の重視」「[[平和運動]]の推進」<ref name="shin"/>という七つの目標を達成するための「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開が呼びかけられた。
==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。 ==出典== {{reflist}} ==外部リンク== *[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 新日本建設国民運動要領 国立国会図書館サイト内] {{DEFAULTSORT:しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう}} [[Category:日本の政治]] [[Category:日本の政治史]] [[Category:社会運動]]

'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]の[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。 ==概要== [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。
「要領」の推進者として[[森戸辰男]]文部大臣の名があげられる<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html 森戸辰男、人と思想 小池聖一]</ref>。
「要領」には、「祖国再建をめざす積極的な意欲と情熱にみちた力強く新しい精神」、「自立自救の責任感と相互扶助の友愛心」「生活の窮苦に打ち克つ心構え」、「明日の正しく明るく力強い文化日本」<ref name="shin"/>など、国民の精神・文化の発揚、自己啓発を強く呼びかけるフレーズが並んだ。
「要領」では、「勤労意欲の高揚」「[[友愛]]協力の発揮」「自立精神の養成」「社会正義の実現」「合理的・民主的な生活慣習の確立」「[[芸術]]、[[宗教]]および[[スポーツ]]の重視」「[[平和運動]]の推進」<ref name="shin"/>という七つの目標を達成するための「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開が呼びかけられた。
==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。 ==出典== {{reflist}} ==外部リンク== *[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 新日本建設国民運動要領 国立国会図書館サイト内] {{DEFAULTSORT:しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう}} [[Category:日本の政治]] [[Category:日本の政治史]] [[Category:社会運動]]

'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]の[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。戦前の国家主義・日本主義を標榜する運動とは別。 ==概要== [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。

'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]の[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。戦前の国家主義・日本主義を標榜する運動とは別。 ==概要== [[Image:Katayama cabinet.jpg|thumb|200px|新日本建設国民運動要領を閣議決定した片山内閣の閣僚たち]] [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。
「要領」の推進者として[[森戸辰男]]文部大臣の名があげられる<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html 森戸辰男、人と思想 小池聖一]</ref>。
「要領」には、「祖国再建をめざす積極的な意欲と情熱にみちた力強く新しい精神」、「自立自救の責任感と相互扶助の友愛心」「生活の窮苦に打ち克つ心構え」、「明日の正しく明るく力強い文化日本」<ref name="shin"/>など、国民の精神・文化の発揚、自己啓発を強く呼びかけるフレーズが並んだ。
「要領」では、「勤労意欲の高揚」「[[友愛]]協力の発揮」「自立精神の養成」「社会正義の実現」「合理的・民主的な生活慣習の確立」「[[芸術]]、[[宗教]]および[[スポーツ]]の重視」「[[平和運動]]の推進」<ref name="shin"/>という七つの目標を達成するための「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開が呼びかけられた。
「要領」にもとづいて、社会教育連合会（現「全日本社会教育連合会」）編の『新日本建設の道』（[[国立印刷局|印刷局]]、[[1948年]]）<ref>[http://opac.ndl.go.jp/recordid/000000844513/jpn 48010249 国立国会図書館]</ref>といった書籍も出版された。「第1章」を「わが国経済の現状」として、「第2章」以下は、七つの目標から各章が構成された。「勤労意欲の高揚」を戦前最後の[[ILO]]東京支局長、戦後最初の[[中央労働委員会]]事務局長を歴任した鮎沢巌、「友愛・協力の発揮」を教育者の[[笹森順造]]、「自立精神の養成」を経済学者の[[高橋誠一郎]]、「社会正義の実現」を[[森戸辰男]]、「合理的民主的な生活慣習の確立」を婦人運動家の[[奥むめお]]、「芸術・宗教及スポーツの重視」を文筆家の[[新居格]]、「平和運動の推進」を[[1946年]]から新日本建設キリスト運動を開始していた[[賀川豊彦]]が書いた。
==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。 ==出典== {{reflist}} ==外部リンク== *[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 新日本建設国民運動要領 国立国会図書館サイト内] {{DEFAULTSORT:しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう}} [[Category:日本の政治]] [[Category:日本の政治史]] [[Category:社会運動]]

'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]を中心とする[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。戦前の国家主義・日本主義を標榜する運動とは別。 ==概要== [[Image:Katayama cabinet.jpg|thumb|200px|新日本建設国民運動要領を閣議決定した片山内閣の閣僚たち]] [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。
「要領」の推進者として[[森戸辰男]]文部大臣の名があげられる<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html 森戸辰男、人と思想 小池聖一]</ref>。

「要領」では、「勤労意欲の高揚」「[[友愛]]協力の発揮」「自立精神の養成」「社会正義の実現」「合理的・民主的な生活慣習の確立」「[[芸術]]、[[宗教]]および[[スポーツ]]の重視」「[[平和運動]]の推進」<ref name="shin"/>という七つの目標を達成するための「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開が呼びかけられた。
「要領」にもとづいて、社会教育連合会（現「全日本社会教育連合会」）編の『新日本建設の道』（[[国立印刷局|印刷局]]、[[1948年]]）<ref>[http://opac.ndl.go.jp/recordid/000000844513/jpn 48010249 国立国会図書館]</ref>といった書籍も出版された。「第1章」を「わが国経済の現状」として、「第2章」以下は、七つの目標から各章が構成された。「勤労意欲の高揚」を戦前最後の[[ILO]]東京支局長、戦後最初の[[中央労働委員会]]事務局長を歴任した鮎沢巌、「友愛・協力の発揮」を教育者の[[笹森順造]]、「自立精神の養成」を経済学者の[[高橋誠一郎]]、「社会正義の実現」を[[森戸辰男]]、「合理的民主的な生活慣習の確立」を婦人運動家の[[奥むめお]]、「芸術・宗教及スポーツの重視」を文筆家の[[新居格]]、「平和運動の推進」を[[1946年]]から新日本建設キリスト運動を開始していた[[賀川豊彦]]が書いた。
==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。 ==出典== {{reflist}} ==外部リンク== *[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 新日本建設国民運動要領 国立国会図書館サイト内] {{DEFAULTSORT:しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう}} [[Category:日本の政治]] [[Category:日本の政治史]] [[Category:社会運動]]

'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]を中心とする[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。戦前の国家主義・日本主義を標榜する運動とは別。 ==概要== [[Image:Katayama cabinet.jpg|thumb|200px|新日本建設国民運動要領を閣議決定した片山内閣の閣僚たち]] [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。

企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。 ==出典== {{reflist}} ==外部リンク== *[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 新日本建設国民運動要領 国立国会図書館サイト内] {{DEFAULTSORT:しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう}} [[Category:日本の政治]] [[Category:日本の政治史]] [[Category:社会運動]]

'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]を中心とする[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。戦前の国家主義・日本主義を標榜する運動とは別。 ==概要== [[Image:Katayama cabinet.jpg|thumb|200px|新日本建設国民運動要領を閣議決定した片山内閣の閣僚たち]] [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。
「要領」の推進者として[[森戸辰男]]文部大臣の名があげられる<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html 森戸辰男、人と思想 小池聖一]</ref>。
「要領」には、「祖国再建をめざす積極的な意欲と情熱にみちた力強く新しい精神」、「自立自救の責任感と相互扶助の友愛心」「生活の窮苦に打ち克つ心構え」、「明日の正しく明るく力強い文化日本」<ref name="shin"/>など、国民の精神・文化の発揚、自己啓発を強く呼びかけるフレーズが並んだ。
「要領」では、「勤労意欲の高揚」「[[友愛]]協力の発揮」「自立精神の養成」「[[社会正義]]の実現」「合理的・民主的な生活慣習の確立」「[[芸術]]、[[宗教]]および[[スポーツ]]の重視」「[[平和運動]]の推進」<ref name="shin"/>という七つの目標を達成するための「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開が呼びかけられた。
「要領」にもとづいて、社会教育連合会（現「全日本社会教育連合会」）編の『新日本建設の道』（[[国立印刷局|印刷局]]、[[1948年]]）<ref>[http://opac.ndl.go.jp/recordid/000000844513/jpn 48010249 国立国会図書館]</ref>といった書籍も出版された。「第1章」を「わが国経済の現状」として、「第2章」以下は、七つの目標から各章が構成された。「勤労意欲の高揚」を戦前最後の[[国際労働機関|ILO]]東京支局長、戦後最初の[[中央労働委員会]]事務局長を歴任した鮎沢巌、「友愛・協力の発揮」を教育者の[[笹森順造]]、「自立精神の養成」を経済学者の[[高橋誠一郎]]、「社会正義の実現」を[[森戸辰男]]、「合理的民主的な生活慣習の確立」を婦人運動家の[[奥むめお]]、「芸術・宗教及スポーツの重視」を文筆家の[[新居格]]、「平和運動の推進」を[[1946年]]から新日本建設キリスト運動を開始していた[[賀川豊彦]]が書いた。
==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。
==出典== {{reflist}}
==外部リンク== *[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 新日本建設国民運動要領 国立国会図書館サイト内]

「要領」には、「祖国再建をめざす積極的な意欲と情熱にみちた力強く新しい精神」、「自立自救の責任感と相互扶助の友愛心」「生活の窮苦に打ち克つ心構え」、「明日の正しく明るく力強い文化日本」<ref name="shin"/>など、国民の精神・文化の発揚、自己啓発を強く呼びかけるフレーズが並んだ。
「要領」では、「勤労意欲の高揚」「[[友愛]]協力の発揮」「自立精神の養成」「[[社会正義]]の実現」「合理的・民主的な生活慣習の確立」「[[芸術]]、[[宗教]]および[[スポーツ]]の重視」「[[平和運動]]の推進」<ref name="shin"/>という七つの目標を達成するための「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開が呼びかけられた。
「要領」にもとづいて、社会教育連合会（現「全日本社会教育連合会」）編の『新日本建設の道』（[[国立印刷局|印刷局]]、[[1948年]]）<ref>[http://opac.ndl.go.jp/recordid/000000844513/jpn 48010249 国立国会図書館]</ref>といった書籍も出版された。「第1章」を「わが国経済の現状」として、「第2章」以下は、七つの目標から各章が構成された。「勤労意欲の高揚」を戦前最後の[[国際労働機関|ILO]]東京支局長、戦後最初の[[中央労働委員会]]事務局長を歴任した鮎沢巌、「友愛・協力の発揮」を教育者の[[笹森順造]]、「自立精神の養成」を経済学者の[[高橋誠一郎]]、「社会正義の実現」を[[森戸辰男]]、「合理的民主的な生活慣習の確立」を婦人運動家の[[奥むめお]]、「芸術・宗教及スポーツの重視」を文筆家の[[新居格]]、「平和運動の推進」を[[1946年]]から新日本建設キリスト運動を開始していた[[賀川豊彦]]が書いた。
==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。
==出典== {{reflist}}
==外部リンク== *[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 新日本建設国民運動要領 国立国会図書館サイト内]
{{DEFAULTSORT:しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう}} [[Category:占領下の日本の政治]] [[Category:戦後日本の社会運動]]

revision.comment: [[Category:占領下の日本の政治]]を除去; [[Category:1947年の日本の政治]]を追加 ([[WP:HotCat|HotCat]]使用)
revision.text:
'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]を中心とする[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。戦前の国家主義・日本主義を標榜する運動とは別。 ==概要== [[Image:Katayama cabinet.jpg|thumb|200px|新日本建設国民運動要領を閣議決定した片山内閣の閣僚たち]] [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[http://www.ndl.go.jp/horei_jp/kakugi/txt/txt00802.htm 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。
「要領」の推進者として[[森戸辰男]]文部大臣の名があげられる<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html 森戸辰男、人と思想 小池聖一]</ref>。
「要領」には、「祖国再建をめざす積極的な意欲と情熱にみちた力強く新しい精神」、「自立自救の責任感と相互扶助の友愛心」「生活の窮苦に打ち克つ心構え」、「明日の正しく明るく力強い文化日本」<ref name="shin"/>など、国民の精神・文化の発揚、自己啓発を強く呼びかけるフレーズが並んだ。
「要領」では、「勤労意欲の高揚」「[[友愛]]協力の発揮」「自立精神の養成」「[[社会正義]]の実現」「合理的・民主的な生活慣習の確立」「[[芸術]]、[[宗教]]および[[スポーツ]]の重視」「[[平和運動]]の推進」<ref name="shin"/>という七つの目標を達成するための「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開が呼びかけられた。
「要領」にもとづいて、社会教育連合会（現「全日本社会教育連合会」）編の『新日本建設の道』（[[国立印刷局|印刷局]]、[[1948年]]）<ref>[http://opac.ndl.go.jp/recordid/000000844513/jpn 48010249 国立国会図書館]</ref>といった書籍も出版された。「第1章」を「わが国経済の現状」として、「第2章」以下は、七つの目標から各章が構成された。「勤労意欲の高揚」を戦前最後の[[国際労働機関|ILO]]東京支局長、戦後最初の[[中央労働委員会]]事務局長を歴任した鮎沢巌、「友愛・協力の発揮」を教育者の[[笹森順造]]、「自立精神の養成」を経済学者の[[高橋誠一郎]]、「社会正義の実現」を[[森戸辰男]]、「合理的民主的な生活慣習の確立」を婦人運動家の[[奥むめお]]、「芸術・宗教及スポーツの重視」を文筆家の[[新居格]]、「平和運動の推進」を[[1946年]]から新日本建設キリスト運動を開始していた[[賀川豊彦]]が書いた。
==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。

==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。
==出典== {{reflist}}
==外部リンク== *[https://rnavi.ndl.go.jp/politics/entry/bib00802.php 新日本建設国民運動要領 国立国会図書館サイト内]

revision.comment: 外部リンクの修正 http:// -> https:// (opac.ndl.go.jp) ([[Wikipedia:Bot|Bot]]による編集)
revision.text:
'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]を中心とする[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。戦前の国家主義・日本主義を標榜する運動とは別。 ==概要== [[Image:Katayama cabinet.jpg|thumb|200px|新日本建設国民運動要領を閣議決定した片山内閣の閣僚たち]] [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[https://rnavi.ndl.go.jp/politics/entry/bib00802.php 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。
「要領」の推進者として[[森戸辰男]]文部大臣の名があげられる<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html 森戸辰男、人と思想 小池聖一]</ref>。
「要領」には、「祖国再建をめざす積極的な意欲と情熱にみちた力強く新しい精神」、「自立自救の責任感と相互扶助の友愛心」「生活の窮苦に打ち克つ心構え」、「明日の正しく明るく力強い文化日本」<ref name="shin"/>など、国民の精神・文化の発揚、自己啓発を強く呼びかけるフレーズが並んだ。
「要領」では、「勤労意欲の高揚」「[[友愛]]協力の発揮」「自立精神の養成」「[[社会正義]]の実現」「合理的・民主的な生活慣習の確立」「[[芸術]]、[[宗教]]および[[スポーツ]]の重視」「[[平和運動]]の推進」<ref name="shin"/>という七つの目標を達成するための「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開が呼びかけられた。
「要領」にもとづいて、社会教育連合会（現「全日本社会教育連合会」）編の『新日本建設の道』（[[国立印刷局|印刷局]]、[[1948年]]）<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 48010249 国立国会図書館]</ref>といった書籍も出版された。「第1章」を「わが国経済の現状」として、「第2章」以下は、七つの目標から各章が構成された。「勤労意欲の高揚」を戦前最後の[[国際労働機関|ILO]]東京支局長、戦後最初の[[中央労働委員会]]事務局長を歴任した[[鮎沢巌]]、「友愛・協力の発揮」を教育者の[[笹森順造]]、「自立精神の養成」を経済学者の[[高橋誠一郎]]、「社会正義の実現」を[[森戸辰男]]、「合理的民主的な生活慣習の確立」を婦人運動家の[[奥むめお]]、「芸術・宗教及スポーツの重視」を文筆家の[[新居格]]、「平和運動の推進」を[[1946年]]から新日本建設キリスト運動を開始していた[[賀川豊彦]]が書いた。
==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。
==出典== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
'''新日本建設国民運動'''（しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう）とは、[[日本社会党]]を中心とする[[片山哲]]内閣によって呼びかけられた文化上・精神上・生活上の改善・啓発運動である。戦前の国家主義・日本主義を標榜する運動とは別。 ==概要== [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|新日本建設国民運動要領を閣議決定した片山内閣の閣僚たち]] [[1947年]][[6月20日]]の閣議決定「'''新日本建設国民運動要領'''」<ref name="shin">[https://rnavi.ndl.go.jp/cabinet/bib00802.html 日本と世界の議会・法令・官庁資料(議会官庁資料室) 新日本建設国民運動要領 国会図書館]</ref>によって基本方針が決定された。
「要領」の推進者として[[森戸辰男]]文部大臣の名があげられる<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html 森戸辰男、人と思想 小池聖一]</ref>。
「要領」には、「祖国再建をめざす積極的な意欲と情熱にみちた力強く新しい精神」、「自立自救の責任感と相互扶助の友愛心」「生活の窮苦に打ち克つ心構え」、「明日の正しく明るく力強い文化日本」<ref name="shin"/>など、国民の精神・文化の発揚、自己啓発を強く呼びかけるフレーズが並んだ。
「要領」では、「勤労意欲の高揚」「[[友愛]]協力の発揮」「自立精神の養成」「[[社会正義]]の実現」「合理的・民主的な生活慣習の確立」「[[芸術]]、[[宗教]]および[[スポーツ]]の重視」「[[平和運動]]の推進」<ref name="shin"/>という七つの目標を達成するための「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開が呼びかけられた。
「要領」にもとづいて、社会教育連合会（現「全日本社会教育連合会」）編の『新日本建設の道』（[[国立印刷局|印刷局]]、[[1948年]]）<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 48010249 国立国会図書館]</ref>といった書籍も出版された。「第1章」を「わが国経済の現状」として、「第2章」以下は、七つの目標から各章が構成された。「勤労意欲の高揚」を戦前最後の[[国際労働機関|ILO]]東京支局長、戦後最初の[[中央労働委員会]]事務局長を歴任した[[鮎沢巌]]、「友愛・協力の発揮」を教育者の[[笹森順造]]、「自立精神の養成」を経済学者の[[高橋誠一郎]]、「社会正義の実現」を[[森戸辰男]]、「合理的民主的な生活慣習の確立」を婦人運動家の[[奥むめお]]、「芸術・宗教及スポーツの重視」を文筆家の[[新居格]]、「平和運動の推進」を[[1946年]]から新日本建設キリスト運動を開始していた[[賀川豊彦]]が書いた。
==動員の対象== 動員の対象とされたのは、各界代表者、[[学校]]、学校関係団体、[[青年団]]、婦人会、[[労働組合]]、農民組合、各種産業団体、法曹団体、文化団体、[[宗教団体]]などであった<ref name="shin"/>。[[新聞]]、[[雑誌]]、[[放送]]、[[映画]]、[[演劇]]、[[音楽]]、[[文芸]]などのメディアの動員も求められた<ref name="shin"/>。「要領」は、全国各地に'''国民運動協議会'''を開催し、[[公民館]]を拠点として、「地方の実情」に応じた「'''新生活国民運動'''」の展開を促した。
企業や労働組合には、「勤労者の教育を徹底し、職域を中心とする[[リクリエーション]]運動を促進する」ことが求められ、後にこれが企業による[[家族計画]]への干渉に繋がっていったとの説がある<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html 1950-60年代における企業による家族管理— 新生活運動の展開に即して —柳井 郁子（日本学術振興会特別研究員）同時代史学会 News Letter（第6号）2005年4月]</ref>。
「要領」は、[[憲法普及会]]と同会を主体とした、[[日本国憲法]]に基づく政治教育運動の推進をうたっていたが、[[憲法普及会]]が[[1947年]]末に解散したため、実らなかった。一方、'''新生活運動推進協議会'''という地域組織が各地につくられ、片山内閣が退陣し、[[保守合同]]の後も、地域や企業によっては住民ぐるみ、従業員ぐるみの運動を展開していくことになった<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 熊本県教育委員会 心豊かな熊本を創る運動推進協議会の沿革]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html 新生活運動協議会 よしの祐子 日本女性の動き年表]</ref>。
==出典== {{reflist}}
==外部リンク== *[https://rnavi.ndl.go.jp/cabinet/bib00802.html 新日本建設国民運動要領 国立国会図書館サイト内]
{{DEFAULTSORT:しんにほんけんせつこくみんうんどう}} [[Category:1947年の日本の政治]] [[Category:戦後日本の社会運動]] [[Category:片山哲]]

revision.comment: 外部リンクの修正 (rnavi.ndl.go.jp) ([[Wikipedia:Bot|Bot]]による編集)

revision.comment: Removed redirect to [[en:New Life Movement]]
revision.text:
Not to be confused with the Chinese [[en:New Life Movement]] this just has a similar name [[category:new pages]]
'''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the [[Japan Socialist Party]]. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements.
== Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō}} [[Category:1947 in Japanese politics]] [[Category:Postwar Japanese social movements]] [[Category:Tetsu Katayama]]

== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō}} [[Category:1947 in Japanese politics]] [[Category:Postwar Japanese social movements]] [[Category:Tetsu Katayama]] [[Category:新日本建設国民運動]][[ja:新日本建設国民運動]]

Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements.

Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

revision.text:
Not to be confused with the Chinese [[en:New Life Movement]] this just has a similar name [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:New Life Movement]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:New Life Movement]]'''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the [[Japan Socialist Party]]. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements.
== Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

'''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the [[Japan Socialist Party]]. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements. [[en:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-New Life Movement (Japan)-qq]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] == Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]

Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]

'''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the [[Japan Socialist Party]]. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements. [[en:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-New Life Movement (Japan)-qq]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] == Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>

The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>

== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.

The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]

This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements. The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>

Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.

Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

'''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the [[Japan Socialist Party]]. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements. [[en:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] <!--New Life Movement (Japan)-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] == Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō}} [[Category:1947 in Japanese politics]] [[Category:Postwar Japanese social movements]] [[Category:Tetsu Katayama]] [[Category:新日本建設国民運動]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:新日本建設国民運動]]

This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements. [[en:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] <!--New Life Movement (Japan)-->[[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] == Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>

== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>

This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements. [[en:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] <!--New Life Movement (Japan)-->[[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] == Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the [[Japan Socialist Party]]. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements. [[en:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] <!--New Life Movement (Japan)-->[[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] == Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō}} [[Category:1947 in Japanese politics]] [[Category:Postwar Japanese social movements]] [[Category:Tetsu Katayama]] [[Category:新日本建設国民運動]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:新日本建設国民運動]] [[Category:New]]

Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the [[Japan Socialist Party]]. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements. [[en:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:New Life Movement (Japan)]][[Category:qq]] <!--New Life Movement (Japan)-->[[Category:New Life Movement (Japan)]] == Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
