Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[:en:Toyohiko Kagawa|Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[:en:1946|1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[:en:labor unions|labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[:en:Religious organizations|Religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[:en:community centers|community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[:en:1947|1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[:en:conservative coalition|conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements.
== Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[:en:1948|1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[:en:Toyohiko Kagawa|Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[:en:1946|1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[:en:labor unions|labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[:en:Religious organizations|Religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[:en:community centers|community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[:en:1947|1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[:en:conservative coalition|conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]
{{translated page|ja|新日本建設国民運動|version=98994793|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[:en:labor unions|labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[:en:Religious organizations|Religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[:en:community centers|community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11502371]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the [[:en:Japan Socialist Party|Japan Socialist Party]]. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements.
== Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in [[:en:1948|1948]] by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist [[:en:Toyohiko Kagawa|Toyohiko Kagawa]], who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in [[:en:1946|1946]], on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[:en:labor unions|labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[:en:Religious organizations|Religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[:en:community centers|community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the [[:en:Constitution of Japan|Constitution of Japan]] through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of [[:en:1947|1947]] hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent [[:en:conservative coalition|conservative coalition]], the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]
{{translated page|ja|新日本建設国民運動|version=98994793|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]]

Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent {{ill|conservative coalition|en|conservative coalition|WD=Q5163103|lt=conservative coalition}}, the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]]
'''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the {{ill|Japan Socialist Party|en|Japan Socialist Party|WD=Q1144146|lt=Japan Socialist Party}}. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements.
== Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948}} by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist {{ill|Toyohiko Kagawa|en|Toyohiko Kagawa|WD=Q731925|lt=Toyohiko Kagawa}}, who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in {{ill|1946|en|1946|WD=Q18610|lt=1946}}, on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[:en:labor unions|labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[:en:Religious organizations|Religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[:en:community centers|community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan}} through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of {{ill|1947|en|1947|WD=Q5263|lt=1947}} hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent {{ill|conservative coalition|en|conservative coalition|WD=Q5163103|lt=conservative coalition}}, the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}

Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent {{ill|conservative coalition|en|conservative coalition|WD=Q5163103|lt=conservative coalition}}, the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:新日本建設国民運動]]
'''Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō''' (新日本建設国民運動) was a cultural, spiritual, and lifestyle improvement movement initiated under the [[Katayama Tetsu Cabinet]] centered on the {{ill|Japan Socialist Party|en|Japan Socialist Party|WD=Q1144146|lt=Japan Socialist Party}}. This movement was distinct from pre-war nationalism and Japanism movements.
== Overview == [[Image:Tetsu Katayama Cabinet 19470524.jpg|thumb|200px|Members of the Katayama Cabinet who adopted the guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō.]] On [[June 20, 1947]], the Cabinet decided on the "'''Guidelines for the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō'''."<ref name="shin">[https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 "Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines," National Diet Library]</ref>
The movement's proponent was [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]], the Minister of Education.<ref>[http://home.hiroshima-u.ac.jp/hua/public/kiyou001/koike.html "Tatsuo Morito: His Life and Thought" by Seiichi Koike]</ref>
The "Guidelines" included phrases calling for the revival of national spirit and culture, such as "a new and dynamic spirit filled with the active will and passion for rebuilding the homeland," "a sense of responsibility for self-help and mutual aid," "resilience against life's hardships," and "a properly bright and robust cultural Japan of tomorrow."<ref name="shin"/>
The movement aimed to achieve seven objectives: "enhancing the willingness to work," "demonstrating fraternal cooperation," "cultivating a spirit of independence," "realizing social justice," "establishing rational and democratic lifestyle practices," "emphasizing art, religion, and sports," and "promoting peace movements."<ref name="shin"/>
Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948}} by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist {{ill|Toyohiko Kagawa|en|Toyohiko Kagawa|WD=Q731925|lt=Toyohiko Kagawa}}, who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in {{ill|1946|en|1946|WD=Q18610|lt=1946}}, on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[:en:labor unions|labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[:en:Religious organizations|Religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[:en:community centers|community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan}} through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of {{ill|1947|en|1947|WD=Q5263|lt=1947}} hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent {{ill|conservative coalition|en|conservative coalition|WD=Q5163103|lt=conservative coalition}}, the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/db/cabinet/s21_22/bib00802 Shin Nihon Kensetsu Kokumin Undō Guidelines, National Diet Library]

Books such as *The Path to Rebuilding Japan* (published in {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948|1=1948}} by the Social Education Federation, now known as the All Japan Federation for Social Education) were published based on these guidelines.<ref>[https://ndlonline.ndl.go.jp/#!/detail/R300000001-I000000844513-00 *The Path to Rebuilding Japan*, National Diet Library]</ref> The book was organized into chapters corresponding to the seven objectives. Authors included: * [[Iwao Ayuzawa]], former ILO Tokyo branch director, on "Enhancing the Willingness to Work." * Educator [[Junzō Sasamori]] on "Demonstrating Fraternal Cooperation." * Economist [[Seiichirō Takahashi]] on "Cultivating a Spirit of Independence." * [[:en:Tatsuo Morito|Tatsuo Morito]] on "Realizing Social Justice." * Women's rights activist [[Mumeo Oku]] on "Establishing Rational and Democratic Lifestyle Practices." * Writer [[Itaru Arai]] on "Emphasizing Art, Religion, and Sports." * Christian activist {{ill|Toyohiko Kagawa|en|Toyohiko Kagawa|WD=Q731925|lt=Toyohiko Kagawa|1=Toyohiko Kagawa}}, who had initiated the Shin Nihon Kensetsu Christian Movement in {{ill|1946|en|1946|WD=Q18610|lt=1946|1=1946}}, on "Promoting Peace Movements."
== Mobilization Targets == The movement targeted various sectors, including representatives from different fields, schools, school organizations, [[youth associations]], women’s associations, [[:en:labor unions|labor unions]], agricultural unions, industry organizations, legal groups, cultural groups, and [[:en:Religious organizations|Religious organizations]].<ref name="shin"/> Media such as newspapers, magazines, broadcasting, films, theater, music, and literature were also called upon to participate.<ref name="shin"/> The "Guidelines" encouraged the establishment of *National Movement Councils* nationwide, using [[:en:community centers|community centers]] as hubs for developing *New Lifestyle National Movements* tailored to local circumstances.
Companies and labor unions were encouraged to "thoroughly educate workers and promote workplace-centered recreation activities," a development that later extended to corporate involvement in family planning.<ref>[http://jachs.hp.infoseek.co.jp/newsletter/006/NL006.html "Corporate Family Management in the 1950s-60s: New Lifestyle Movements" by Ikuko Yanai, Japan Contemporary History Society Newsletter, Issue 6]</ref>
Although the "Guidelines" called for political education based on the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|WD=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}} through the [[Constitution Promotion Society]], the dissolution of the society at the end of {{ill|1947|en|1947|WD=Q5263|lt=1947|1=1947}} hindered progress. Nevertheless, regional organizations such as *New Lifestyle Movement Promotion Councils* were established, and even after the dissolution of the Katayama Cabinet and the subsequent {{ill|conservative coalition|en|conservative coalition|WD=Q5163103|lt=conservative coalition|1=Conservative coalition}}, the movement persisted at the local and corporate levels.<ref>[http://www.higo.ed.jp/kyouikuiinkai/kiji2/pub/default.phtml?p_id=764 Kumamoto Prefecture Board of Education: History of the Movement to Create a Heartful Kumamoto]</ref><ref>[http://www.jca.apc.org/~yuko_y/chron02/p55_8.html "New Lifestyle Movement Councils," Yuko Yoshino]</ref>
